data.title: The Hidden Robot
data.description:
By Rafael Silva

Be it Wall-E, R2-D2, or Rosie from The Jetsons, most readers grew up with sci-fi molding what a robot looks like and is capable of doing. Technology has now come so far that we no longer need to rely on Hollywood to imagine this part of the future for us. News shows and viral videos exposing us to actual hardware being developed today carry that mantle now.

We’ve moved from the imaginary to the technically possible, but most of our knowledge remains founded on what is fun to watch, rather than what is making a true difference to society. After all, Boston Dynamic’s dancing dogs and humanoids get more views than Fanuc's (FANUY) robots, which are involved in the production of a significant amount of all vehicles globally and have shipped over a million units.

The Invisible Army Behind the Scenes

There’s an army of robots marching behind the scenes to enable today’s lifestyle. We can divide those into industrial and service robots. FANUC is an example of the first. The industrial robot market is the larger and vastly more mature of the two. It contributes to the manufacturing of electronics, automotives, machinery, as well as behind-the-scenes logistics automation happening in warehouses across the globe. This makes it less visible to the average person.

Service robots, on the other hand, are much more likely to coexist with us. We may interact with service robots as a user or as a “colleague” of sorts. We would see these in areas like last-mile logistics, professional cleaning, or healthcare, for example. This proximity to a larger part of the population still doesn’t provide the average person with an insight into the growth or the prevalence of the technology. How many of us are aware that the number of industrial robots in operation has increased 216% in the last 10 years? It’s gone from 1.5 million in 2014 to 4.7 million in 2024.

Service Robots and Labor Realities

The latest available data from the International Federation for Robotics (IFR) estimates that 2024 saw a total of 199,000 professional service robots installed, a 9% increase from the previous year. That number, while robust in itself, does not tell the whole story. Within the service robots category, the installation of medical robots increased by 91% to 16,700 units. Meanwhile, professional cleaning robots saw their installations increase by 34%, and search and rescue increased by 19%. These numbers give us an indication of two things. First of all, what tasks the current technology is capable of performing; second, and most importantly, what industries face the biggest labor shortages.

To determine whether a task is automated, ask whether a robot would be more efficient in the long term. Essentially, this automation deployment happens where economics meet both functionality and scalability. Importantly, new drivers for automation are coming into play on the demand side. There, we have an aging population and a lack of skilled workers as primary drivers. The World Health Organization (WHO) is projecting a global shortage of over 10 million healthcare workers by 2030, for example. That is no doubt part of the reason why healthcare is making a strong push for automation today.

Healthcare as a Window Into Automation

Healthcare provides us with a great window into how these forces are interacting. 2024 saw an increase of 610% in the installation of diagnostics & medical laboratory analysis robots (IFR data). These robots allow for precise and efficient execution of time-consuming and error-prone tasks, 24/7. Rehabilitation & non-invasive therapy (+106%) as well as surgical (+41%) robots are another example of segments where the machines are quickly providing high returns on investment. They bring either immediate efficiency improvements or enable the available workforce to do a better job in an environment less prone to burnout.

The existing technology continuously improves, fueled by advances in materials and software. Meanwhile, this same progress is opening doors to new types of robots. Last year’s addition to the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO), Ondas Holdings (ONDS), is deploying mine swiping as well as border patrol robots, among many other solutions for autonomous strategic asset protection. Drones were brought into focus during the ongoing Ukrainian conflict. They very quickly moved from niche consumer market to an important part of defense, agriculture and even transportation.

Humanoids and the Household Challenge

Humanoids also exploded into the scene in 2025. They brought the enormous potential of deployment in a world built for humans and follow a trend of integrating standalone technologies to create synergies. Cleaning robots can also provide an element of security. Hotel robots capable of carrying food or towels are also capable of providing directions or answering questions about hotel amenities. Essentially, we are entering an age of general-purpose robots.

Going into 2026, it is important to note that the accelerated growth seen in the robotics industry has been against a backdrop of uncertain political policies and high cost of capital. While neither of these is conducive to high capex investments like robotic installations, there have been billions of capital commitments for reshoring in the U.S. and abroad. Industrial use cases are increasing and diversifying, and adoption among the service industry is growing rapidly. We are seeing fast adoption of models such as robotics-as-a-service (RaaS) as well as increasingly sophisticated financing models. Of note, in the United States we will see continued benefit of 100% bonus depreciation of capex investments in the first year.

One place where growth has been limited is the home. As of today, robotic vacuums remain the only product to successfully make it into our houses. Humanoids may change that. Incredible pent-up and anticipated demand for multipurpose robots capable of doing chores around the house remains. The challenges to that remain the same they have been from the beginning: availability, safety and cost.

The breakthroughs driving humanoid performance don’t exist in isolation. Vision systems, manipulation algorithms, and AI models transfer readily across form factors, suggesting households may adopt multiple specialized robots rather than just a single general-purpose unit. The question shifts from when to how many.

The Geopolitical Race for Leadership

Going forward, we see the robotic industry as one poised for rapid growth. The question of where that growth will come from - and what companies will lead it - is a much more complex one. At this point, China is undoubtedly leading the adoption race, with over half of all industrial installations taking place in the country. They also have about 100 companies actively developing humanoid robots. The U.S., on the other hand, is undergoing the third phase of its National Robotics Initiative (NRI-3.0). It announced its Genesis Mission late in 2025, described by the administration as a “Manhattan project for AI and robotics.” This creates a complex environment where governmental policy is as important as technological development by individual companies.

While growth may be likely, picking the winners requires expertise. That is why the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO) relies on a strategic advisory board including the actual architects of the robotics revolution. People like Dr. Daniela Rus, the Director of MIT CSAIL and Dr. Henrik Christensen, professor of computer science at UC San Diego, represent part of a team of market and thought leaders which help the strategy identify the invisible robots that are actually building the future.

ROBO is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO), the L&G ROBO Global Robotics and Automation UCITS ETF (ROBO.LN), and the Global X ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO.AU).

data.title: Jabil: Rating Upgrade On Better Revenue Visibility And Competitive Position
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to Jabil Inc. (JBL) previously, with my worry being whether JBL can execute given the capacity bottlenecks to justify its premium valuation. So far, the developments have changed my view. Data center architecture is shifting in JBL's favor, revenue visibility has improved meaningfully, and management has taken steps to remove the key bottlenecks that limited execution. I upgrade to buy.

Structural Shifts Favor Jabil's Model

A few months back, my view was less bullish on JBL because I saw execution risk. While AI spending was accelerating, JBL's growth was constrained by capacity (from power to labor to even chips). But things have changed. There is a structural shift in data center architecture that materially improves JBL's positioning, I believe. The industry is moving away from traditional top-of-rack switches toward independent switching racks, which is a better design to support GPU-heavy workloads. This is a massive change that effectively rewired the value chain in the data center, which is where JBL's years of investments in its Intelligent Infrastructure strategy (built around complex rack-level assembly, power integration, and large-scale electromechanical systems) will start to shine.

As you can imagine, the complexity and value proposition change. JBL is no longer competing within the "box," where it is really a competition of assembling the right components to ensure the "box" works. When we are talking about "rack," the competition is on who can best integrate, test, and deliver full rack-level solutions at scale. That capability is way harder to replicate vs. box-level manufacturing. The comparison is simple to understand. When you build a switch, you really only need a workbench and a conveyor line. When you integrate a rack for modern AI workloads, you are effectively assembling a high-performance computing cluster inside a cabinet. That requires reinforced concrete floors to support the weight (can exceed 2,500 lbs per rack), and you also need liquid cooling infrastructure running through the facility (to test and make sure the rack doesn't melt) and megawatts of power capacity (to conduct burn-in and validation testing). Most ODMs that focused only on the "box" don't have the infrastructure for this. It is not like they can simply turn this capability on overnight. They are literally restrained by physical requirements, which creates a real, physical barrier to entry. This is why I think JBL is in a very solid position to win a ton of share (or in other words, its addressable opportunity got larger).

Not only that, but JBL also has an advantage when it comes to partnership with Nvidia (NVDA). Given that JBL has already had a manufacturing partnership with NVDA for a while now, it is one of the scaled players outside of East Asia that can meet this ramp as NVDA aims to reduce geopolitical risk. From NVDA's perspective, that makes JBL's harder to replace, which effectively embeds JBL deeper into NVDA's product roadmap.

Revenue Visibility Improves

Another reason I stayed on the sidelines previously was visibility. At the time, backlog was building, but it was not translating into recognized revenue fast enough to justify the valuation. I wanted to wait until demand showed up in the income statement before I got interested in turning bullish. JBL did just that, and it is stronger than I expected.

On visibility, management raised FY2026 AI revenue guidance to $12.1 billion, up from $11.2 billion previously. That is near an entire billion dollars of upgrade, which sends a strong message that the backlog is going to translate into revenue growth and suggests the constraints I worried about (component availability and customer acceptance delays) are likely easing. This materially improves forward visibility. Two reasons I think the guide is plausible. First is that management guidance has historically been accurate (~1.5% delta on average).

The second is because JBL has effectively "bought" the certainty through the Hanley Energy acquisition. This literally solves the single biggest friction point in the bear case (capacity constraints). I used to be in that camp worrying about JBL's needs to secure the power and cooling supply, which could swing intra-quarter numbers, as JBL may not be able to meet the client's (especially hyperscalers) aggressive delivery schedules. Hanley Energy is the piece that solves this puzzle. For reference, Hanley Energy specializes in power management solutions for data centers. By bringing this capability in-house, Jabil has vertically integrated the most critical choke point (today) in the AI infrastructure buildout. In the past, JBL wasn't able to recognize revenue because of supply constraints, as it relies on third-party suppliers. If a power unit vendor was late, JBL couldn't ship the rack, and revenue slipped to the next quarter. By vertically integrating, JBL now has better control of the timing of the entire assembly process. This should reduce the risk of shipment delays, making the revenue timing much more predictable.

While the price tag is huge, so much so that it required JBL to issue a $1 billion senior notes offering split between 2029 and 2033 maturities, I believe the strategic rationale outweighs the cost of capital. I see this as a relatively cheap way to finance a capability that can enable JBL to deliver the $12.1 billion revenue target. If JBL had not made this move, they would continue to be at the mercy of third-party vendors during a period of peak demand. From an offensive standpoint, by integrating Hanley, JBL not only protects its delivery schedule but also captures more margin dollars per rack. It also enhances JBL's value proposition to hyperscalers, who are desperate for vendors that can provide turnkey solutions.

Valuation

Given that my concerns are basically addressed, I now believe JBL is worth a buy rating. You can see from the chart above that consensus has upgraded their estimates (adj. EPS growth up ~800 bps vs. my update in September '25). The market is clearly resonating with my views, as multiples have gone higher, now at 20x vs. ~19x NTM PE back then. But I think it makes sense, and I believe multiple can stay at this level so long as JBL can continue to ride on this AI infrastructure build-out phase.

JBL has almost never missed consensus adj. EPS estimates before, for reference, and I don't think it will anytime soon given the visibility it has. Looking out into the next few years, JBL is expected to generate ~$15.9 adj. EPS. If multiple stays at 20x, the stock is easily worth more than $300, or more than 20% upside from here.

Investment Risk

Integrating the Hanley acquisition may be harder than expected. Any friction in merging power products could ultimately lead to a delay in deliveries and, hence, revenue recognition. While I think it makes sense to use debt, it does increase leverage, which could weigh on multiples if JBL doesn't deliver. Also, any pause/delay in hyperscaler AI capex spending would also hurt JBL's revenue growth cadence.

Conclusion

My view for JBL is a buy rating. The structural tailwinds from rack-level data center architecture, deeper ties with NVDA, and materially improved revenue visibility have addressed my previous concerns. The Hanley Energy acquisition directly removes the most important constraint, making delivery timing and revenue conversion far more visible. While leverage has increased, I believe the trade-off is justified.

data.title: Grid Dynamics: Focus On Inorganic Moves (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm becoming bullish on Grid Dynamics Holdings (GDYN).

GDYN's external growth prospects are healthy, considering recent news flow and the company's financial strength.

My prior May 8, 2024, write-up touched on its better-than-anticipated turnover and below-consensus bottom line for 1Q24.

Positive On Alliances With Tech Heavyweights

Last month, GDYN revealed a "multi-year strategic collaboration agreement (SCA) with Amazon (AMZN) Web Services (AWS)."

Ecosystem Members

My take is that this latest development will help maintain the group's competitiveness.

The GDYN-AMZN tie-up spans one and a half decades. The company's website states that its "AWS start-up kit" can "reduce time-to-value for generative AI initiatives by up to 60%."

You would worry about the firm losing substantial business without Amazon's support.

A newly added partner is Nvidia (NVDA).

NVDA-GDYN "Intralogistics Optimization Starter Kit" Introduced Last Year

I think that its allies are important in many ways.

At the 3Q25 briefing, GDYN highlighted "joint marketing and sales campaigns" with "the hyperscalers." These initiatives allow it to capitalize on the huge sales teams of Microsoft (MSFT) Azure and Google (GOOG) Cloud to attract customers.

Also, it has gained acceptance from reputable research organizations in recent years, as outlined below. That was made possible by the co-developed solutions. Its slides indicated that the "40+ proprietary accelerators", mostly created together with GOOG/AMZN, can "reduce Fortune 1000 implementation time from 12+ months to 6-9 months."

GDYN's Awards

GDYN saw its list of collaborators expand from a mid-single-digit to the low teens in the last 36 months.

That led to the proportion of the enterprise's partner-linked topline rising from under a percent to 18% (source: Q3 call) during the same period.

As per S&P Capital IQ, the sell-side is projecting that GDYN's revenue will accelerate from FY22-25's +9.9% to FY2025-2028's +13.5%. Analysts also estimate a +140bps (basis points) EBITDA-to-sales improvement between '24 and '28.

The market's optimism is reasonable in my view.

The company has a goal of raising the partner-related contribution to 25%-30%. That should enhance its future turnover.

Separately, GDYN indicated at the 3Q2025 meeting it often acts as "Client Zero" by utilizing partner-supported AI tools to enhance "efficiency on our own business process." This points to lower expenses going forward.

Acquisitions Are In The Cards

Morgan Stanley (MS) published a report (not publicly accessible) titled "Investment Banks Deal Dashboard" on Dec. 26, 2025. MS's research suggested that worldwide "announced M&A volume" surged +65% YoY in 4Q25. The same metric for North America is even higher at +119%.

I believe that this M&A wave is sustainable.

An early-January 2026 SA News piece cited a "Citizens Financial Group (CFG) survey" of "U.S.-based companies" and "PE firms." The results suggest "90% expect deal flow will rise or hold steady this year, due to improving economic conditions and attractive sector-wide valuations."

There are internal factors compelling GDYN to follow this trend.

The firm's net cash as of Sept. 30, 2025, amounted to $0.33 billion. That was equivalent to 45% of its market cap.

It has been free cashflow positive for every single year since FY2017 based on S&P Capital IQ data. I don't see the need for GDYN to hold such a sizeable amount of surplus capital.

GDYN's Past Takeovers

Also, it hasn't done any deals in the past 16 months. The previous ones were mainly bolt-ons, with the investees boasting annual revenues of under $0.05B.

GDYN stressed at the 3Q analyst briefing that "M&A continues to take priority in our capital allocation strategy." It also mentioned that the focus is on "acquisitions that strategically enhance our capabilities, geographic presence and industry verticals."

I infer from the management's commentary that the acquisition drought could be over soon. It is also likely to look at more sizable targets, which can boost its growth outlook. The cash pile available for deployment should imply a greater deal appetite.

The 13.5% top-line CAGR projection referenced in the preceding section might be surpassed in the presence of inorganic growth.

You Should Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

If key allies choose to work with GDYN's rivals instead, there's a risk of market share loss.

The company might continue to engage in cash-hoarding. In that case, investors will most probably assign a hefty valuation discount to penalize the stock for poor capital allocation.

My Concluding Remarks

I've revised GDYN's rating from "Hold" to "Buy."

I like how it's expanding through external means.

The stock is still valued using a "Price-to-Earnings Growth/PEG" method as per the May '24 article.

GDYN trades at 18.5x FY26 P/E. The consensus FY25-28 EPS CAGR estimate is +22.1% (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Its potential upside is +20% (22.1/18.5) pegged to 1xPEG, justifying a favorable investment view.

data.title: Hartford Core Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities advanced in the fourth quarter, registering a sizable return for 2025. Markets were bolstered by robust corporate earnings, resilient consumer spending, and solid economic growth, although anxiety about lofty valuations in the technology sector and concerns surrounding massive AI spending drove capital toward value/cyclical areas of the market. The cooling labor market and worries about upside inflation risks also kept the markets on edge. In the third quarter, the US economy grew at the fastest pace in two years; GDP surged 4.3% annually, primarily fueled by robust consumer spending but also aided by a rebound in exports, business investment on equipment and AI, and increased government spending on defense. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) reduced interest rates by 50 basis points (bps)* during the quarter. Policymakers were divided on their outlook for interest rates amid considerable uncertainty about inflation and the labor market, with the Fed’s median dot-plot projection showing only one rate cut in 2026.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Core Equity Fund (I share) underperformed the S&P 500 Index during the quarter.
  • Security selection detracted from performance driven by weak selection in information technology, materials, and communications services. This was partially offset by strong selection in industrials, healthcare, and consumer staples.
  • Sector allocation, a residual of our bottom-up stock selection process, also detracted from performance driven by an underweight to healthcare. This was partially offset by an underweight to information technology.
  • Top relative detractors included an overweight in Pool Corp (consumer discretionary), not owning Micron Technology (information technology), and an overweight in AutoZone (consumer discretionary).

Positioning & Outlook

  • The AI infrastructure capex cycle will remain a key 2026 theme, but is evolving from broad enthusiasm toward a more discerning focus on capital intensity and funding needs. The year should also mark a shift from pure AI enablers to companies best positioned to translate AI adoption into revenue growth, efficiency, and margin expansion across sectors. The US consumer warrants continued monitoring as affordability pressures persist. Wage growth has moderated, labor-market cooling is evident, and spending patterns are shifting. While early 2026 may benefit from seasonal tax-related stimulus, cumulative inflation continues to weigh on households, with autos and housing remaining key drivers of sentiment and electoral outcomes. We remain disciplined and focused on companies with improving quality and fundamental momentum. We favor businesses with pricing power, strong balance sheets, and operational agility—attributes that help navigate tariff passthroughs and policy volatility.
  • At the end of the period, our largest overweights were to consumer staples and utilities. The largest underweights were to healthcare and information technology.
*Basis point is a unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1% and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security
